meta.title: ConservationLint: Noether-Theoretic Program Analysis for Scientific Computing
meta.core_insight: Bridge between Noether's theorem and program analysis via Lie-symmetry detection in computational intermediate representations

theorems.name: Tagged Modified Equation Theorem
theorems.statement: For a liftable program fragment P with phase space declaration Φ, the tagged modified equation E_P^{tag} = ∂u/∂t + L_P^{(k)}[u] + O(h^{k+1}) preserves conservation laws under computational transformation iff the provenance tag algebra T maintains Lie bracket structure modulo discretization error bounds ε(h,k).
theorems.proof_strategy: Constructive proof via BCH expansion with tagged intermediate operations. Show that discretization-aware tagged algebra preserves infinitesimal generators of symmetry groups up to controlled error bounds.
theorems.novelty_assessment: Engineering contribution rather than fundamental theorem. Novel application of established backward error analysis to conservation law detection.
theorems.what_is_known: Backward error analysis for numerical methods, Lie group theory for PDEs, provenance tracking in program analysis
theorems.what_is_new: Integration of provenance algebra with Lie-theoretic structure, discretization-aware conservation checking
theorems.complexity: O(n^k * d^2) where n=program size, k=BCH order, d=phase space dimension
assumptions: Program fragment is liftable to smooth dynamical system
Phase space structure declared by user annotations
Transcendental functions limited to sin/cos/exp/log with exact symbolic representation
BCH expansion converges within computational precision bounds
concrete_examples: Heat equation discretization preserving energy conservation
Wave equation finite difference scheme conserving momentum
theorems.limitations: Provenance tags degenerate at high BCH orders (k≥4), requiring graduated localization
theorems.how_it_enables_artifact: Provides theoretical foundation for detecting when discretization breaks conservation laws in scientific codes

theorems.name: Obstruction Criterion
theorems.statement: Let G be the Lie algebra of infinitesimal symmetries of continuous system S, and let G_d be the discrete symmetries of program P. Then P violates conservation law iff ∃g ∈ G such that (a) g corresponds to conserved quantity Q via Noether's theorem, (b) g ∉ G_d (obstruction exists), (c) ||π_d(g) - g||_op > ε_tol (projection error exceeds tolerance), (d) obstruction is not eliminable within Lie-algebraic framework.
theorems.proof_strategy: Semi-algebraic geometry approach using Groebner bases for obstruction ideal computation. Decidability follows from quantifier elimination over real closed fields.
theorems.novelty_assessment: Decidable criterion for conservation violation, but exponential complexity limits practical scope
theorems.what_is_known: Noether's theorem, Lie group discretization theory, semi-algebraic decision procedures
theorems.what_is_new: Computational obstruction criterion scoped to Lie-algebraic framework, algorithmic approach to conservation violation detection
theorems.complexity: EXPSPACE-complete in general, but tractable for small phase space dimension p≤3 and BCH order k≤3
assumptions: Obstruction ideal has finite Groebner basis
Real closed field axioms apply to coefficient domain
Necessity claim limited to Lie-algebraic framework only
concrete_examples: Symplectic structure violation in Hamiltonian systems
Energy non-conservation in discretized wave equations
Mass conservation failure in advection schemes
theorems.limitations: Exponential blowup for p≥4, necessity limited to specific algebraic framework
theorems.how_it_enables_artifact: Enables automated detection of fundamental conservation violations beyond simple numerical checks

theorems.name: Liftable Fragment Characterization
theorems.statement: A program fragment P is liftable to smooth dynamical system iff (1) all operations preserve differentiable structure on declared phase space, (2) control flow is statically analyzable with bounded nesting depth, (3) transcendental functions are restricted to {sin, cos, exp, log} with exact symbolic representation, (4) memory access patterns respect phase space geometry.
theorems.proof_strategy: Constructive characterization via IR analysis. Show that liftable fragments admit smooth extension to continuous phase space with controlled approximation error.
theorems.novelty_assessment: Clean formalization of practical scope limitations
theorems.what_is_known: Smooth manifold theory, static program analysis, numerical analysis convergence theory
theorems.what_is_new: Precise characterization of when discrete programs admit continuous interpretation for conservation analysis
theorems.complexity: Linear in program size for syntactic checks, polynomial for semantic liftability verification
assumptions: Phase space has smooth manifold structure
User provides accurate phase space annotations
Approximation errors remain within floating-point precision
concrete_examples: Finite difference stencils on regular grids
theorems.limitations: Excludes adaptive mesh refinement, discontinuous solutions, arbitrary control structures
theorems.how_it_enables_artifact: Defines precise scope of applicability for conservation analysis techniques

definitions.name: Liftable Fragment
definitions.formal_statement: A program fragment P is liftable if there exists a smooth dynamical system (M, F) where M is the declared phase space manifold and F: M → TM such that P approximates the flow of F within specified error bounds ε(h) where h is the discretization parameter.
definitions.decidability: Decidable via syntactic analysis + semantic verification

definitions.name: Conservation-Aware IR
definitions.formal_statement: Extended intermediate representation Γ = (V, E, T, Φ, S) where V=variables, E=expressions, T=provenance tags, Φ=phase space declaration, S=symmetry annotations. Each operation op ∈ E carries tag t ∈ T tracking conservation-relevant transformations.
definitions.decidability: Constructible from source via static analysis
definitions.coverage_estimate: Handles all liftable fragments with phase space declarations
failure_taxonomy: Tag algebra overflow at high BCH orders

definitions.name: Differential Symbolic Slicing
definitions.formal_statement: For conservation law Q = ∫ ρ(u) dx where ρ is conserved density, compute symbolic slice S_Q = {stmt ∈ P | stmt influences dQ/dt} using backwards dataflow analysis extended with differential structure awareness.
definitions.decidability: Computable via extended reaching definitions analysis
definitions.coverage_estimate: Complete for liftable fragments with declared conservation laws
failure_taxonomy: Higher-order conservation laws requiring extended calculus

definitions.name: Phase Space Declaration
definitions.formal_statement: Annotation system Φ: Variables → PhaseSpaceStructure where PhaseSpaceStructure ∈ {Euclidean(n), Symplectic(2n), Riemannian(M,g), ProductSpace(Φ₁×Φ₂)}. Declares geometric structure required for Lie symmetry analysis.
definitions.coverage_estimate: Requires manual annotation for each scientific code

definitions.name: Provenance Tag Algebra
definitions.formal_statement: Algebraic structure T = (Tag, ⊕, ⊗, [·,·]) where Tag tracks conservation-relevant operations, ⊕ combines tags linearly, ⊗ represents composition, [·,·] is Lie bracket operation preserving infinitesimal symmetry structure.
definitions.coverage_estimate: Exact for BCH orders k≤3, approximate for k≥4
failure_taxonomy: Numerical precision loss in bracket computations

algorithms.name: Hybrid IR Extraction
algorithms.input_spec: Python AST + phase space annotations Φ
algorithms.output_spec: Conservation-aware IR Γ with tagged operations
algorithms.pseudocode_summary:
1. Parse AST to identify computational kernels
2. Extract liftability-preserving operations
3. Attach provenance tags to each operation
4. Build phase space mapping from annotations
5. Validate geometric consistency
algorithms.complexity: O(n log n) where n = AST size
algorithms.data_structures: Annotated AST, Symbol table with geometric types, Tag lattice
algorithms.implementation_language: Python with SymPy for symbolic manipulation
algorithms.bottleneck: Phase space consistency checking and tag algebra construction

algorithms.name: Lie Symmetry Analysis
algorithms.input_spec: Conservation-aware IR Γ, declared conservation laws Q
algorithms.output_spec: Infinitesimal generators g ∈ G, discrete symmetries G_d
algorithms.pseudocode_summary:
1. Extract differential operators from IR
2. Compute prolongations using chain rule
3. Solve determining equations for symmetry generators
4. Identify discrete approximations G_d
5. Check preservation of conservation laws
algorithms.complexity: O(d³ · k²) where d = phase space dimension, k = derivative order
algorithms.data_structures: Jet space coordinates, Differential operator matrices, Symmetry generator basis
algorithms.implementation_language: SymPy with custom Lie algebra extensions
algorithms.bottleneck: Solving nonlinear determining equations for large phase spaces

algorithms.name: Provenance-Tagged BCH Expansion
algorithms.input_spec: Tagged IR operations, symmetry generators G, BCH order k
algorithms.output_spec: Tagged modified equation E_P^{tag} with error bounds
algorithms.pseudocode_summary:
1. Compute BCH series to order k
2. Track provenance through each term
3. Evaluate commutators with tag algebra
4. Accumulate discretization error bounds
5. Generate tagged modified equation
algorithms.complexity: O(n^k · |T|) where n = operation count, |T| = tag space size
algorithms.data_structures: BCH coefficient tree, Tag composition tables, Error bound intervals
algorithms.bottleneck: Exponential growth of BCH terms and tag combinations

algorithms.name: Conservation Obstruction Check
algorithms.input_spec: Continuous symmetries G, discrete symmetries G_d, tolerance ε_tol
algorithms.output_spec: Obstruction certificate or conservation preservation proof
algorithms.pseudocode_summary:
1. Compute projection π_d: G → G_d
2. Calculate operator norm ||π_d(g) - g||_op
3. Check if projection error > ε_tol
4. Generate obstruction ideal using Groebner bases
5. Apply semi-algebraic decision procedure
algorithms.complexity: EXPSPACE-complete general case, polynomial for small p,k
algorithms.data_structures: Lie algebra matrices, Groebner basis, Obstruction ideal generators
algorithms.bottleneck: Groebner basis computation for high-dimensional obstruction ideals

algorithms.name: Causal Localization via Differential Slicing
algorithms.input_spec: Conservation violation certificate, tagged IR Γ
algorithms.output_spec: Ranked list of violation-causing program statements
algorithms.pseudocode_summary:
1. Identify violated conservation law Q
2. Compute differential slice S_Q backwards from Q
3. Rank statements by tag influence on violation
4. Apply graduated localization for high-order terms
5. Generate causal explanation trace
algorithms.complexity: O(n · d) where n = program size, d = dependency depth
algorithms.data_structures: Dataflow graph, Influence ranking, Causal trace tree
algorithms.implementation_language: Python with networkx for graph analysis
algorithms.bottleneck: Precise influence quantification through complex tag interactions

algorithms.name: Dynamic Ablation Analysis
algorithms.input_spec: Suspected violation sites, test suite T
algorithms.pseudocode_summary:
1. Generate program variants by ablating suspected sites
2. Run test suite on each variant
3. Measure conservation law adherence
4. Compare predicted vs observed violations
5. Refine localization based on empirical results
algorithms.complexity: O(|S| · |T| · C) where |S| = suspected sites, |T| = test cases, C = conservation check cost
algorithms.data_structures: Program variant generator, Test execution framework, Conservation metrics
algorithms.implementation_language: Python with pytest for test orchestration
algorithms.bottleneck: Test suite execution time for large codebases

algorithms.name: End-to-End ConservationLint Pipeline
algorithms.input_spec: Python source code + phase space annotations
algorithms.output_spec: Conservation violation report with localized causes
algorithms.pseudocode_summary:
1. Extract conservation-aware IR
2. Perform Lie symmetry analysis
3. Compute tagged BCH expansion
4. Check for conservation obstructions
5. Localize violations causally
6. Validate via dynamic ablation
7. Generate comprehensive report
algorithms.complexity: O(n^k · d³ + EXPSPACE_obstruction) dominated by obstruction checking
algorithms.data_structures: Pipeline state machine, Intermediate result cache, Report generator
algorithms.bottleneck: Obstruction checking for complex phase spaces and high BCH orders

benchmark_suite.total_coverage: Representative sample of scientific computing domains

baselines.name: Manual Expert Review
baselines.description: Domain experts manually identify conservation violations

baselines.name: Symbolic Computation
baselines.description: Computer algebra systems for conservation checking
baselines.limitations: Manual setup required, no automated program analysis

primary: Precision: True violations / (True violations + False positives)
Recall: True violations / (True violations + False negatives)
Localization accuracy: Correct violation causes / Total reported causes
secondary: False positive rate by conservation law type

kill_criteria: Analysis time > 10 minutes per KLOC for typical codes
Phase space annotation overhead > 20% of original code
Unable to handle codes with phase space dimension > 6
falsifiability_conditions: ConservationLint misses >40% of manually identified violations
False positive rate exceeds 50% across benchmark suite
Expert evaluation shows localization is consistently wrong
Tool fails on >30% of liftable benchmark programs
Obstruction checking proves intractable for realistic phase spaces
threats_to_validity: Benchmark selection bias toward amenable problem types
Expert ground truth may miss subtle violations
Phase space annotations may not reflect true geometric structure
Limited evaluation of non-liftable code fragments
Scalability testing constrained by computational resources

kill_gates.criteria: Basic IR extraction + simple conservation checking working
Symmetry analysis produces meaningful results on toy problems
kill_gates.risk_if_failed: Theoretical framework too complex for practical implementation

kill_gates.criteria: Performance meets minimum thresholds on evaluation suite
kill_gates.risk_if_failed: Approach not competitive with simpler alternatives

kill_gates.criteria: Handles realistic scientific codes within reasonable time/memory
kill_gates.risk_if_failed: Computational complexity prohibitive for practical use

binding_conditions_status.obstruction_criterion_decidability: COMPLETE - semi-algebraic approach established with complexity bounds
binding_conditions_status.phase_space_annotation_system: IN_PROGRESS - syntax defined, semantic validation needs work
binding_conditions_status.bch_expansion_convergence: COMPLETE - error bounds established for k≤3

language_choices.rationale: Rich ecosystem for symbolic computation, AST manipulation, scientific libraries
language_choices.symbolic_backend: SymPy for core symbolic manipulation, SageMath for advanced algebra
language_choices.numerical_backend: NumPy/SciPy for linear algebra, mpmath for extended precision
language_choices.testing_framework: pytest with hypothesis for property-based testing

implementation_strategy.quality_assurance: Code review, automated testing, benchmark validation, expert consultation

assumptions_registry.statement: Users can accurately annotate phase space structure
assumptions_registry.mitigation: Provide phase space inference tools, validation checks, common pattern library

assumptions_registry.statement: Scientific codes are primarily liftable to smooth dynamical systems
assumptions_registry.mitigation: Empirical validation on benchmark suite, clear scope documentation
assumptions_registry.impact_if_wrong: Limited applicability, many codes excluded from analysis

assumptions_registry.statement: BCH expansion converges adequately for orders k≤3
Obstruction checking remains tractable for realistic phase spaces (p≤6)
assumptions_registry.impact_if_wrong: Tool unusable on realistic problems due to computational complexity

assumptions_registry.statement: Provenance tags preserve sufficient information for causal localization
assumptions_registry.mitigation: Graduated localization strategy, tag compression techniques
assumptions_registry.impact_if_wrong: Poor violation localization, reduced diagnostic value

assumptions_registry.statement: Transcendental functions limited to {sin,cos,exp,log} covers most scientific codes
assumptions_registry.mitigation: Extensible symbolic engine, approximate handling of special functions
assumptions_registry.impact_if_wrong: Many codes use excluded transcendentals, reducing coverage

assumptions_registry.statement: Static analysis can identify most conservation-relevant operations
Domain experts can provide ground truth for evaluation

adversarial_findings.finding: T1 is engineering contribution, not fundamental theorem
adversarial_findings.resolution: Reframe as practical formalization rather than theoretical breakthrough

adversarial_findings.finding: T2 necessity claim overstated - only applies within Lie-algebraic framework
adversarial_findings.resolution: Scope necessity to specific framework, acknowledge limitations

adversarial_findings.finding: Obstruction checking has exponential complexity for realistic problems
adversarial_findings.resolution: Focus on tractable cases (p≤3, k≤3), develop heuristic approximations

adversarial_findings.finding: Provenance tags degenerate at high BCH orders
adversarial_findings.resolution: Implement graduated localization strategy, document limitations

adversarial_findings.finding: Phase space annotations create significant user burden
adversarial_findings.resolution: Develop inference tools, provide template library, focus on common patterns

adversarial_findings.finding: Liftable fragment definition excludes many realistic codes
adversarial_findings.resolution: Clearly document scope, provide fallback analysis for non-liftable fragments

adversarial_findings.finding: Evaluation may be biased toward favorable problem types
adversarial_findings.resolution: Include challenging benchmarks, negative cases, stress tests

key_references: Olver (1993) - Applications of Lie Groups to Differential Equations
Stephani (1989) - Differential Equations: Their Solutions Using Symmetries
what_is_known.established_results: Deep connection between continuous symmetries and conserved quantities, systematic methods for finding symmetries of differential equations

key_references: Hairer et al. (2006) - Geometric Numerical Integration
Stuart & Humphries (1998) - Dynamical Systems and Numerical Analysis
what_is_known.established_results: Modified equation approach for analyzing discretization effects, structure-preserving numerical methods

key_references: Nielson et al. (1999) - Principles of Program Analysis
Muchnick (1997) - Advanced Compiler Design and Implementation
what_is_known.established_results: Dataflow analysis, abstract interpretation, program slicing, provenance tracking

key_references: Stodden et al. (2016) - Implementing Reproducible Research
Hatton & Roberts (1994) - How Accurate is Scientific Software?
what_is_known.established_results: Software engineering challenges in scientific computing, verification and validation methodologies

what_is_new.contribution: Integration of Lie-theoretic conservation analysis with program analysis
what_is_new.prior_work: No existing tools combine Noether's theorem with automated program analysis for conservation checking

what_is_new.contribution: Provenance-tagged BCH expansion for discretization-aware conservation checking
what_is_new.prior_work: BCH expansion known, but not combined with program provenance for conservation analysis

what_is_new.contribution: Computational obstruction criterion for conservation violations
what_is_new.prior_work: Obstruction theory exists in geometry, but not applied to computational conservation checking

what_is_new.contribution: Liftable fragment characterization for scientific programs
what_is_new.prior_work: Some work on continuous interpretation of discrete programs, but not systematic characterization

what_is_new.contribution: Differential symbolic slicing for causal localization
what_is_new.prior_work: Program slicing well-established, but not extended with differential structure awareness

related_tools.name: SymPy
related_tools.relation: Foundation for symbolic computation, extended with conservation-specific analysis
related_tools.differentiator: ConservationLint adds Lie-theoretic analysis and program integration

related_tools.name: SageMath
related_tools.relation: Advanced algebraic computation for obstruction checking
related_tools.differentiator: ConservationLint focuses specifically on conservation laws rather than general algebra

related_tools.name: FEniCS/Firedrake
related_tools.relation: Finite element frameworks with some conservation awareness
related_tools.differentiator: ConservationLint analyzes existing code rather than providing simulation framework

related_tools.name: Pylint/mypy
related_tools.differentiator: ConservationLint provides domain-specific conservation law analysis

theoretical_foundations: Differential geometry and Lie group theory
Numerical analysis and backward error analysis
Semi-algebraic geometry and real algebraic geometry
Variational calculus and conservation law theory
open_questions_addressed: How can Noether's theorem be computationally applied to existing scientific codes?
What is the relationship between discretization errors and conservation law violations?
Can conservation violations be localized causally in complex scientific programs?
What classes of scientific programs admit systematic conservation analysis?
